sentences.grammarNote: مَرحَباً (marhaban) = hello — an accusative verbal noun used as an exclamatory greeting. Arabic greetings are often frozen accusative forms: أَهلاً (ahlan, welcome), شُكراً (shukran, thanks), عَفواً (ʿafwan, pardon). أنا سيث is a nominal sentence without a verb: 'I Seth' = 'I am Seth.' The copula كون (to be) is omitted in the present tense.

صَدِيق جِيف (ṣadīq Jiff) is an إِضَافَة (iḍāfa, possessive construct): صَدِيق (friend) + جيف (Jeff) = Jeff's friend. The first noun loses its definite article (if any); the second noun defines the first. No word for 'of' is used. صَدِيق (ṣadīq, friend) from root ص-د-ق (ṣ-d-q, truth/sincerity): صَدَقَ (to be truthful), صِدق (truthfulness).

أَعِيش (aʿīsh, I live) — Form I of root ع-ي-ش (ʿ-y-sh, to live). The present-tense first-person prefix is أَ (a-): أَعِيش (I live), أَعمَل (I work), أَذهَب (I go). This prefix is one of the defining features of Arabic present-tense conjugation. The verb is intransitive here, followed by the preposition فِي (in).

sentences.base_language: I work as a software engineer.
sentences.grammarNote: كـ (ka-) as a prefix means 'as' or 'like'. كمهندس = as an engineer. This prefix attaches directly to the noun.

sentences.base_language: I have been learning Arabic for six months.
sentences.grammarNote: منذ (mundhu) = since / for (with duration). Used with the present tense in Arabic to describe ongoing actions that started in the past, unlike English which uses the present perfect.

sentences.base_language: Arabic is difficult but I enjoy it.
sentences.grammarNote: العربية صعبة (al-ʿarabiyya ṣaʿba) is a nominal sentence: definite subject + indefinite predicate adjective, no verb needed. لكنَّني (lakinnanī, but I) — لكنَّ (but/however, emphatic) + suffix ني (me/I). اِستَمتَعَ بـ (istamtaʿa bi-, to enjoy) — Form X of م-ت-ع (to be pleasant): اِستَمتَعَ بها = enjoyed it (feminine suffix بِها).

sentences.base_language: My favorite part is learning new words.
sentences.target_language: الجزء المفضل لدي هو تعلم الكلمات الجديدة.
sentences.romanized: al-juz' al-mufaddal ladayya huwa ta'allum al-kalimat al-jadida.
sentences.literal: The-part the-favorite to-me is learning the-words the-new.
sentences.grammarNote: الجُزء المُفَضَّل (al-juzʾ al-mufaḍḍal, the favourite part) — definite noun + definite adjective, both with الـ. المُفَضَّل is the passive participle Form II of ف-ض-ل (to favour): the preferred one. لَدَيَّ (ladayya, to me/I have) — emphatic form of عِندي. هُو (huwa) = is (the copula, used here for emphasis in a formal equational sentence).

sentences.base_language: I practice every day for thirty minutes.
sentences.target_language: أتدرب كل يوم لمدة ثلاثين دقيقة.
sentences.romanized: atadarrab kull yawm li-mudat thalathin daqiqa.
sentences.literal: I-practice every day for-duration thirty minute.
sentences.grammarNote: أَتَدَرَّب (atadarrab, I practice) — Form V of root د-ر-ب (d-r-b, to train/practice): Form V تَدَرَّبَ = to train oneself. كُلَّ يَوم (kull yawm, every day) — كُلَّ (every/all) in the accusative before an indefinite noun. لِمُدَّة (li-muddat, for a duration of) — a fixed phrase for expressing duration: لِمُدَّة ثَلاثِين دَقِيقَة = for thirty minutes.

sentences.base_language: I also listen to Arabic music.
sentences.grammarNote: أَستَمِع إِلى (astami', I listen to) — Form VIII of س-م-ع (s-m-ʿ, to hear): اِستَمَعَ إِلى = to listen to (attentively). The preposition إِلى (to) is obligatory with this verb. الموسيقى (al-mūsīqā, music) is a foreign borrowing with a defective ending ى — it does not change for case. العَربِيَّة (al-ʿarabiyya) agrees with الموسيقى in definiteness and gender.

sentences.base_language: One day I want to visit Egypt and Jordan.
sentences.target_language: يوماً ما أريد زيارة مصر والأردن.
sentences.romanized: yawman ma urid ziyarat misr wal-urdunn.
sentences.grammarNote: يَوماً مَا (yawman mā, one day/someday) — يَوماً is the indefinite accusative of يَوم (day), used as a time adverb. The مَا (mā) is an indefinite particle meaning 'some' — this phrase literally means 'some day.' زِيَارَة (ziyāra, visit/visiting) — verbal noun Form I of ز-و-ر (z-w-r, to visit). مِصر (Miṣr, Egypt) and الأُردُن (al-Urdunn, Jordan) are both diptotes (cannot take tanwin).

النَّاطِقُون (an-nāṭiqūn, the speakers — masculine plural) — فَاعِل active participle of ن-ط-ق (n-ṭ-q, to speak/articulate): نَطَقَ (to utter). The sound masculine plural suffix ـُون (-ūn) is used for rational beings: مُعَلِّمُون (teachers), طُلَّاب or طَالِبُون (students). مُرَحِّبُون (muraḥḥibūn, welcoming) — active participle Form II of ر-ح-ب (to welcome).

sentences.base_language: I hope to become fluent one day.
sentences.target_language: آمل أن أصبح طليقاً يوماً ما.
sentences.romanized: amal an asbah taliqan yawman ma.
sentences.literal: I-hope that I-become fluent day some.
sentences.grammarNote: آمُل أَن أُصبِح (āmul an uṣbiḥ, I hope to become) — آمُل from root أ-م-ل (ʾ-m-l, to hope): أَمَل (hope, noun). أَصبَح (aṣbaḥa, to become) is used to express a change of state: أَصبَح + adjective = became/has become. طَلِيقاً (ṭalīqan, fluent — accusative) from root ط-ل-ق (to be free/flowing): طَلِيق اللِّسَان = fluent in speech, literally 'free-tongued.'
